# Verification Proportional to Risk

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Principle

The rigor of verification applied to a change SHOULD be proportional to
the blast radius of that change. A typo fix in documentation does not
need the same review process as a database migration affecting production
data. Teams SHOULD classify changes by risk tier and apply verification
gates accordingly.

## Concept Foundation

- **JOS-C26 (Change as Primary Risk)** — Not all changes carry equal risk.
  A change that can affect one user's display is categorically different
  from a change that can corrupt a payment ledger. Treating all changes
  identically wastes effort on low-risk changes and under-scrutinizes
  high-risk ones. Risk-proportional verification allocates attention where
  it matters most.

## Why This Matters

Uniform verification processes create two failure modes. If the bar is
set high for all changes, low-risk changes accumulate friction and
developers batch them into larger, riskier deployments — exactly the
opposite of what good practice demands. If the bar is set low for all
changes, high-risk changes slip through with inadequate scrutiny. The
solution is a tiered model: low-risk changes move fast with lightweight
checks; high-risk changes slow down for deeper review, staged rollout,
and explicit sign-off.

## Tension and Tradeoffs

Risk classification itself has cost and subjectivity. Teams may disagree
about whether a change is low-risk or high-risk, and misclassification
in either direction has consequences. The principle does not prescribe
a specific risk taxonomy — it asks teams to have one and to use it
consistently. There is also a bootstrapping problem: new systems lack
the historical data to classify risk accurately. In early stages, teams
should err toward higher verification and relax as they build confidence
in their classification.

## 1970/2070 Note

In 1970, every change went through the same batch release process
regardless of risk. In 2070, AI may automatically classify change risk
and route changes to appropriate verification pipelines. The underlying
insight — that verification effort should match consequence severity —
holds in both eras.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain principle extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer principles.

### Framework Connection

Framework §14 (blast radius reasoning with Direction/Reach/Reversibility) provides the risk assessment model that makes verification proportionality operational — the blast radius score determines the verification tier a change must pass through.
